     3→> **Abstract:** Dreams are one of three cognitive cross-cuts in Roko — subsystems that span multiple architectural layers rather than living at a single layer. While Neuro provides persistent memory and Daimon provides emotional context, Dreams provide the offline consolidation mechanism that transforms raw episodic experience into durable semantic knowledge, creative hypotheses, and strategic insights. Dreams are the agent's sleep: a periodic offline process where the agent replays recent episodes, generates counterfactual scenarios, depotentiates emotional charge, discovers cross-episode patterns, and stages new knowledge for waking validation. # Dreams (Offline Learning and Consolidation)

> **Abstract:** Dreams are one of three cognitive cross-cuts in Roko — subsystems that span multiple architectural layers rather than living at a single layer. While Neuro provides persistent memory and Daimon provides emotional context, Dreams provide the offline consolidation mechanism that transforms raw episodic experience into durable semantic knowledge, creative hypotheses, and strategic insights. Dreams are the agent's sleep: a periodic offline process where the agent replays recent episodes, generates counterfactual scenarios, depotentiates emotional charge, discovers cross-episode patterns, and stages new knowledge for waking validation. Dreams are idle-triggered and scheduled — they fire when the agent has capacity, not in response to any mortality or termination signal.

---

## Prerequisites

Before reading this topic, familiarity with the following is helpful:

- **Synapse Architecture**: The 6-trait system (Substrate, Scorer, Gate, Router, Composer, Policy) through which all capabilities flow
- **5-Layer Taxonomy**: L0 Runtime, L1 Framework, L2 Scaffold, L3 Harness, L4 Orchestration
- **Engram**: The content-addressed, scored, decaying unit of cognition (currently named `Signal` in the codebase)
- **Neuro / NeuroStore**: The agent's persistent knowledge base (episodes, insights, heuristics, causal links)
- **Daimon**: The affect engine maintaining PAD (Pleasure-Arousal-Dominance) emotional state vectors

## Table of Contents

| # | Sub-Doc | Description |
|---|---------|-------------|
| 00 | [00-vision-and-dream-as-death-reframe.md](00-vision-and-dream-as-death-reframe.md) | Vision document: what dreams are, why they exist, and the critical reframe from death-triggered to idle-triggered consolidation |
| 01 | [01-three-phase-cycle.md](01-three-phase-cycle.md) | Three-phase dream cycle: NREM replay → REM imagination → Integration staging. Phase descriptions, state machine, resource allocation |
| 02 | [02-nrem-replay.md](02-nrem-replay.md) | NREM replay phase: Mattar-Daw utility formula, four replay modes, cross-episode pattern discovery, emotional modulation, replay fidelity spectrum, SM-2 scheduling, DRL experience replay connections |
| 03 | [03-rem-imagination.md](03-rem-imagination.md) | REM imagination phase: Pearl SCM counterfactuals, Boden's three creativity modes, emotional depotentiation, conceptual blending, imagination validation (GIRL trust-region), imagination budget, world models (DreamerV3/IRIS/Genie) |
| 04 | [04-consolidation-and-staging.md](04-consolidation-and-staging.md) | Integration/consolidation: SQLite staging buffer, confidence ladder, promotion mechanics, temporal decay, safety constraints |
| 05 | [05-dream-evolution.md](05-dream-evolution.md) | EVOLUTION fourth phase: memetic selection, strategy evolution, HDC knowledge recombination, dream-prediction feedback |
| 06 | [06-hdc-counterfactual-synthesis.md](06-hdc-counterfactual-synthesis.md) | HDC counterfactual synthesis: 10,240-bit BSC vectors, XOR binding, majority bundling, K-medoids clustering, counterfactual diversity (DiCE/DPP), plausibility scoring (FACE/LOF/causal consistency) |
| 07 | [07-hypnagogia-engine.md](07-hypnagogia-engine.md) | Hypnagogia engine: Thalamic Gate, Executive Loosener, Dali Interrupt, Homuncular Observer. Stochastic resonance, novelty filtering (Lehman-Stanley), hypnagogia-to-insight pipeline (Wallas/Collins-Loftus) |
| 08 | [08-divergence-and-alpha.md](08-divergence-and-alpha.md) | Alpha convergence problem, three levels of divergence, experiential wisdom thesis, alpha taxonomy |
| 09 | [09-threat-simulation.md](09-threat-simulation.md) | Revonsuo's Threat Simulation Theory, three-tier threat taxonomy, threat rehearsal, gap analysis, systematic threat enumeration (FMEA/FTA/ATLAS), severity assessment (CVSS/DREAD/Bayesian) |
| 10 | [10-hauntology-in-dreams.md](10-hauntology-in-dreams.md) | Derrida hauntology, spectral traces, compound escape from monoculture, knowledge transfer as backup/restore |
| 11 | [11-inner-worlds-and-rendering.md](11-inner-worlds-and-rendering.md) | Visual rendering for each dream phase: NREM theater, REM garden, hypnagogia phosphenes, integration crystallization |
| 12 | [12-sleep-time-compute.md](12-sleep-time-compute.md) | Lin et al. 2025 sleep-time compute, budget allocation, CascadeRouter model selection, Sleepwalker mode |
| 13 | [13-scheduling-and-triggers.md](13-scheduling-and-triggers.md) | Three trigger types (idle, scheduled, manual), frequency adaptation, intensive mode, orchestrator coordination |
| 14 | [14-oneirography.md](14-oneirography.md) | Dream art and creative expression: image generation pipeline, self-appraisal, affect-reactive auctions, extended art forms |
| 15 | [15-cross-system-integration.md](15-cross-system-integration.md) | Integration with Neuro, Daimon, Learn, Compose, Gate, Mesh, Orchestrator, Hypnagogia, Supervisor |
| 16 | [16-implementation-status.md](16-implementation-status.md) | Current code status, implementation plan items, roko-golem dissolution plan, roadmap, open questions |
| 17 | [17-advanced-dream-concepts.md](17-advanced-dream-concepts.md) | Dream sharing across mesh, nightmare detection and containment, persistent dream journals, lucid dream monitoring |

## Cross-References

|-------|-------------|
| `04-neuro/` | Knowledge store that dreams read from and write to |
| `05-daimon/` | Affect engine providing emotional context and receiving depotentiation updates |

| `07-gates/` | Gate pipeline whose results feed dream replay and whose thresholds dreams can update |
| `09-orchestrator/` | Plan execution coordinating with dream scheduling |

| "Spindle-locked ripples mediate memory reactivation," *Nature Communications*, 2024 | First direct human evidence for spindle-ripple coupling in NREM consolidation |
| Tononi lab, *Science*, 2024 | First causal demonstration of Synaptic Homeostasis Hypothesis via SYNCit-K |
| Skenderi et al. (WSCL), *IEEE*, 2024 | Three-phase wake-NREM-REM architecture achieves SOTA continual learning |
| Lin et al., arXiv:2504.13171, 2025 | Sleep-time compute: pre-computed summaries reduce test-time compute 5x |
| Mehrotra et al. (TAP), *NeurIPS*, 2024 | Tree-structured automated red teaming with adaptive branching |
| "MAD-MAX," arXiv, 2025 | Modular attack composition achieving 97% ASR |
| "EEG Microstates in lucid REM," *bioRxiv*, 2025 | Microstate A/G dominance in lucid REM linked to metacognition |
| Ley et al. (GLOBE-CE), *ICML*, 2023 | Global counterfactual explanations via translation vectors |
| "Transport-based Counterfactual Models," *JMLR*, 2024 | Optimal transport counterfactuals respecting data manifold geometry |
| Micheli et al. (Delta-IRIS), *ICML*, 2024 | Efficient delta-encoded world models with context-aware tokenization |
| Google DeepMind (Genie 2/3), 2024-2025 | Generative interactive environments from single image prompts |

---

## Generation Notes

- **Sub-docs produced**: 18 (00 through 17) plus INDEX.md
- **Total line count**: ~10,000+ lines across all sub-docs
- **Key legacy sources consulted**:
  - `bardo-backup/prd/05-dreams/` — dream cycle architecture, consolidation, scheduling
  - `bardo-backup/prd/06-hypnagogia/` — hypnagogia engine, inner worlds, divergence
  - `bardo-backup/prd/22-oneirography/` — creative expression (dream journals, self-appraisal, auctions, extended forms; `02-death-masks.md` skipped per instructions)
  - `refactoring-prd/03-cognitive-layer.md` — three cognitive speeds, dream phases, CLS theory
  - `refactoring-prd/04-systems-and-synapse-traits.md` — Synapse trait mapping for dreams
  - `tmp/implementation-plans/12a-cognitive-layer.md` §G — implementation plan items G1-G8
  - Active code: `roko-dreams/src/runner.rs`, `roko-dreams/src/cycle.rs`, `roko-dreams/src/lib.rs`, `roko-golem/src/dreams.rs`, `roko-golem/src/hypnagogia.rs`, `roko-learn/src/pattern_discovery.rs`, `roko-learn/src/hdc_clustering.rs`
- **Decisions requiring judgment calls**:
  - **Death mask exclusion**: `22-oneirography/02-death-masks.md` was skipped entirely per prompt instructions. All references to death masks in other oneirography docs were reframed or removed.
  - **EVOLUTION phase**: Presented as a fourth dream phase in `05-dream-evolution.md` based on source material, even though the primary cycle is three-phase (NREM/REM/Integration). The EVOLUTION phase is an extension, not a replacement.
  - **Oneirography domain-agnostic reframe**: The legacy oneirography spec was heavily blockchain/NFT-specific. The Roko version presents the core pipeline (dream→image→score) as domain-agnostic, with NFT minting as a blockchain domain extension.
  - **Naming consistency**: All instances of "Golem" → "Agent", "Grimoire" → "Neuro/NeuroStore", "Signal" → "Engram" (with code-name notes where relevant), "Styx" → "Agent Mesh/Mesh", "GNOS" → "KORAI/DAEJI", "Clade" → "Collective/Mesh", "golem.toml" → "roko.toml".
  - **Vitality phases removed**: All references to Thriving/Stable/Conservation/Declining/Terminal behavioral phases have been reframed as continuous budget and knowledge metrics rather than discrete mortality phases.
- **Unresolved tensions**:
  - The `roko-golem` crate still exports `ScaffoldEngine` which `roko-dreams/src/lib.rs` re-exports. This dependency should be removed when `roko-golem` is dissolved.
  - The Daimon (affect engine) is referenced extensively in dream design but is not yet implemented in the codebase.
  - HDC vectors (`bardo-primitives`) are built but not called from the dream subsystem.
- **2025-04 enhancement**: Added 25+ recent citations (2024-2025), Rust structs, pseudocode, test criteria, and configuration parameters throughout

1→# Dreams: Vision and the Dream-as-Death Reframe
     2→
     3→> **Layer**: Cognitive Cross-Cut (injected into L0 Runtime scheduling, L1 Framework agent dispatch, L2 Scaffold context assembly)
     4→>
     5→> **Synapse Traits**: `Substrate` (dream outputs stored in NeuroStore), `Scorer` (replay utility scoring), `Gate` (staging buffer validation), `Policy` (dream scheduling policy)
     6→>
     7→> **Crate**: `roko-dreams` (standalone crate; see `crates/roko-dreams/`)
     8→>
     9→> **Prerequisites**: [01-three-phase-cycle.md](01-three-phase-cycle.md), [../03-neuro/INDEX.md](../06-neuro/INDEX.md) (NeuroStore), [../04-daimon/INDEX.md](../09-daimon/INDEX.md) (affect engine)
    10→
    11→
    12→> **Implementation**: Scaffold
    13→
    14→---
    15→
    16→## What Dreams Are in the Roko Architecture
    17→
    18→Dreams are a **Delta-frequency cognitive process** — the slowest of three cognitive speeds that govern how a Roko agent thinks. Where Gamma (~5–15 seconds) handles reactive perception-action loops and Theta (~75 seconds) handles strategic re-planning, Delta operates on the scale of hours. Dreams are what happens when an agent has nothing urgent to do: it turns inward, replays its past experiences, imagines counterfactual alternatives, and consolidates the results into durable knowledge.
    19→
    20→The term "dream" is not metaphorical. The system implements biological sleep consolidation mechanisms — hippocampal replay, emotional depotentiation, counterfactual simulation — mapped onto LLM-based agents. The research basis is extensive:
    21→
    22→- **Complementary Learning Systems (CLS)**: McClelland et al. (1995) demonstrated that biological brains maintain two learning systems — a fast episodic system (hippocampus) and a slow semantic system (neocortex). Dreams are the bridge: during sleep, episodic memories are replayed and gradually integrated into semantic knowledge. Roko's dream system implements this exact architecture, with the episode log as the fast system and NeuroStore as the slow system.
    23→
    24→- **Sleep-time compute**: Lin et al. (2025, arXiv:2504.11651) showed that dedicating computation to offline processing yields a 5× reduction in test-time compute requirements. The insight is that agents can do significant cognitive work during idle periods, making their waking performance dramatically more efficient.
    25→
    26→- **DreamerV3**: Hafner et al. (2025, Nature) demonstrated that agents learning world models and planning within them can master diverse domains. Roko's dream system uses a similar architecture — the agent builds internal models during dreaming and plans within those models via counterfactual simulation.
    27→
    28→- **Generative Agents**: Park et al. (2023, UIST, arXiv:2304.03442) introduced periodic reflection cycles where agents synthesize higher-order observations from raw experiences. Roko's dream consolidation directly implements this pattern.
    29→
    30→The Roko dream system produces five categories of output:
    31→
    32→| Output | Description | Destination |
    33→|--------|-------------|-------------|
    34→| **Insights** | Patterns extracted from episode replay (e.g., "tasks involving refactoring consistently fail the test gate when the model is under-specified") | NeuroStore via `Substrate` trait |
    35→| **Heuristics** | Promoted insights with 3+ confirmations, becoming actionable rules | NeuroStore + PLAYBOOK.md |
    36→| **Counterfactual strategies** | Novel approaches generated by imagining alternative histories | Staging buffer → NeuroStore on validation |
    37→| **Meta-patterns** | Cross-episode structural similarities discovered via HDC clustering | NeuroStore |
    38→| **Confidence updates** | Strengthened or weakened belief in existing knowledge entries | NeuroStore confidence scores |
    39→
    40→---
    41→
    42→## The Critical Reframe: Dreams Are NOT Death-Triggered
    43→
    44→### What Changed and Why
    45→
    46→The legacy Bardo architecture framed dreams as a response to approaching death. In that system, agents had "stochastic death clocks" — random countdowns to mortality — and dream frequency intensified as death approached. The metaphor was evocative but architecturally harmful:
    47→
    48→1. **It coupled dreams to mortality**, making the dream subsystem depend on a death-clock subsystem that has no place in a general-purpose agent framework.
    49→2. **It made dream frequency unpredictable** — driven by a PRNG countdown rather than by actual cognitive need.
    50→3. **It excluded non-mortal agents** from dreaming, even though every agent benefits from offline consolidation regardless of whether it has a finite lifespan.
    51→4. **It introduced morbid language** throughout the codebase that made the project harder to explain to investors, researchers, and contributors.
    52→
    53→### What Replaces It
    54→
    55→In the Roko architecture, dreams are triggered by two mechanisms:
    56→
    57→**1. Idle-time triggering**: When an agent has no active tasks and has been idle for a configurable threshold (default: 15 minutes), the dream scheduler fires a consolidation cycle. This is the primary trigger. The logic is simple: if you have nothing to do, process what you've learned.
    58→
    59→```rust
    60→pub struct DreamLoopConfig {
    61→    /// Whether automatic dreaming is enabled.
    62→    pub auto_dream: bool,
    63→    /// Idle threshold in minutes before a dream may run.
    64→    pub idle_threshold_mins: u64,
    65→    /// Minimum number of new episodes required before dreaming.
    66→    pub min_episodes_for_dream: usize,
    67→    /// Agent backend used to review the dream batch.
    68→    pub agent: DreamAgentConfig,
    69→}
    70→```
    71→
    72→**2. Scheduled consolidation**: The Delta-frequency scheduler can trigger dreams on a fixed schedule (e.g., every 4 hours, or at specific times of day). This ensures that even busy agents with continuous task queues still get periodic consolidation time.
    73→
    74→Both triggers are configurable in `roko.toml`:
    75→
    76→```toml
    77→[dreams]
    78→auto_dream = true
    79→idle_threshold_mins = 15
    80→min_episodes_for_dream = 5
    81→```
    82→
    83→### What the Legacy System Called "Death Dreams"
    84→
    85→The legacy PRD described several dream-adjacent concepts in death-related terms. Here is the mapping to their Roko equivalents:
    86→
    87→| Legacy Concept | Legacy Description | Roko Equivalent | Roko Description |
    88→|----------------|-------------------|-----------------|------------------|
    89→| **Dream-as-approaching-death** | Dream frequency increases as the agent's death clocks tick down | **Idle-triggered / scheduled consolidation** | Dream frequency is driven by idle time and schedule, not mortality |
    90→| **Terminal dreaming** | Frantic dream cycles every 67 ticks in the Terminal behavioral phase | **Intensive consolidation mode** | When an agent has accumulated many unprocessed episodes (high backlog), consolidation cycles run more frequently — not because of approaching death but because there is more material to process |
    91→| **Death-proximate replay** | Replaying traumatic episodes as the agent nears death | **High-salience replay** | Episodes with high emotional intensity (measured by the Daimon's PAD vectors) are prioritized for replay regardless of the agent's lifecycle state |
    92→| **Thanatopsis dream output** | Final dream producing a "death testament" | **Knowledge export** | An agent can export its accumulated knowledge at any time via user-controlled backup/restore — this is not death-specific |
    93→| **Stochastic death clock** | Random countdown to agent termination that influences dream scheduling | **REMOVED** | No concept of random death in Roko. Agents run until budget exhaustion (economic) or knowledge plateau (epistemic), both of which are continuous metrics, not clocks |
    94→| **Dream urgency from mortality** | Urgency score weighted by proximity to death | **Dream urgency from backlog** | Urgency score weighted by number of unprocessed episodes and time since last consolidation |
    95→
    96→### The Underlying Mechanism Is Preserved
    97→
    98→The computational mechanisms that made the legacy dream system powerful are fully retained. What changes is *when and why* dreams fire, not *what happens during* a dream:
    99→
   100→- **NREM replay** still replays episodes weighted by the Mattar & Daw (2018, Nature Neuroscience) utility formula
   101→- **REM imagination** still generates counterfactuals using Pearl's structural causal models and Boden's three creativity modes
   102→- **Integration** still uses a staging buffer with confidence-gated promotion to permanent knowledge
   103→- **Emotional depotentiation** still reduces arousal markers per Walker & van der Helm (2009, Psychological Bulletin)
   104→- **HDC counterfactual synthesis** still uses 10,240-bit BSC vector permutation operations
   105→
   106→The academic foundations are unchanged. The trigger logic is simplified. The death framing is removed.
   107→
   108→---
   109→
   110→## Where Dreams Fit in the Five-Layer Taxonomy
   111→
   112→Dreams are a **cognitive cross-cut** — they are not confined to a single layer but inject behavior across multiple layers:
   113→
   114→| Layer | Dream Integration |
   115→|-------|-------------------|
   116→| **L0 Runtime** | Dream scheduling: the Delta-frequency scheduler lives in the runtime and decides when to fire dream cycles based on idle time and backlog |
   117→| **L1 Framework** | Agent dispatch: during dreaming, the framework dispatches a review agent (typically a Haiku-class model for NREM, Sonnet-class for REM) to process the dream batch |
   118→| **L2 Scaffold** | Context assembly: dream outputs (insights, heuristics) are injected into future waking prompts via the NeuroStore retrieval pipeline |
   119→| **L3 Harness** | Gate validation: dream-generated hypotheses enter the staging buffer and must pass validation gates before promotion to permanent knowledge |
   120→| **L4 Orchestration** | Dream scheduling is coordinated with task orchestration — the orchestrator determines idle periods and delegates to the Delta scheduler |
   121→
   122→Dreams flow through the Synapse Architecture:
   123→
   124→1. **Substrate**: Episode logs and NeuroStore entries are the substrate from which dreams draw raw material
   125→2. **Scorer**: The Mattar-Daw utility formula scores episodes for replay priority
   126→3. **Gate**: The staging buffer's confidence threshold (0.70) gates promotion of dream-generated hypotheses
   127→4. **Router**: Model routing selects the appropriate LLM tier for each dream phase (cheap models for NREM replay, more capable models for REM imagination)
   128→5. **Composer**: Dream outputs are composed into Engrams (currently `Signal` in the codebase — will be renamed to `Engram` in Tier 0D) and written to NeuroStore
   129→6. **Policy**: The dream scheduling policy observes the Engram stream and emits scheduling decisions
   130→
   131→---
   132→
   133→## Domain-Agnostic Dreaming
   134→
   135→Dreams are **not specific to any domain**. The legacy PRD heavily framed dreams in DeFi terms (replaying liquidation cascades, imagining hedging strategies). In the Roko architecture, dreams are domain-agnostic at the kernel level:
   136→
   137→**Coding agent example**: A coding agent replays past implementation episodes. During NREM, it reviews tasks where the compile gate failed and extracts patterns ("when I modify error types without updating the match arms downstream, the compile gate always fails"). During REM, it imagines: "what if I had run clippy before the test gate?" The counterfactual generates a process improvement heuristic that enters the staging buffer.
   138→
   139→**Research agent example**: A research agent replays past literature review episodes. During NREM, it identifies citation patterns across successful searches. During REM, it combines topics from unrelated research domains to generate novel research hypotheses.
   140→
   141→**Chain agent example**: A chain agent (the `roko-chain` domain plugin) replays past transaction episodes. During NREM, it extracts gas price patterns. During REM, it simulates alternative execution strategies.
   142→
   143→The dream engine provides the consolidation mechanism. The domain plugin provides the episodes and the interpretation. This separation is enforced by the `roko-dreams` crate's lack of dependency on any domain-specific crate.
   144→
   145→---
   146→
   147→## Key Academic Citations for the Dream Subsystem
   148→
   149→The dream system draws from a rich body of research. These citations appear throughout the sub-docs in this topic folder:
   150→
   151→| Paper | Relevance to Dreams |
   152→|-------|-------------------|
   153→| McClelland et al. (1995), Complementary Learning Systems | Foundation: fast episodic + slow semantic memory, bridged by sleep replay |
   154→| Mattar & Daw (2018), Nature Neuroscience, "Prioritized memory access" | NREM replay: utility-weighted episode selection formula |
   155→| Walker & van der Helm (2009), Psychological Bulletin, "Overnight therapy" | Emotional depotentiation: REM sleep reduces emotional charge by 0.3-0.5 per cycle |
   156→| Boden (2004), The Creative Mind: Myths and Mechanisms | REM imagination: three creativity modes (combinational, exploratory, transformational) |
   157→| Pearl (2009), Causality: Models, Reasoning, and Inference | REM counterfactuals: structural causal model three-level framework |
   158→| Hafner et al. (2025), Nature, "DreamerV3" | World model learning during dreaming |
   159→| Park et al. (2023), UIST, arXiv:2304.03442, "Generative Agents" | Periodic reflection and memory synthesis |
   160→| Lin et al. (2025), arXiv:2504.11651, "Sleep-time compute" | 5× reduction in test-time compute via offline processing |
   161→| Revonsuo (2000), Behavioral and Brain Sciences, "Threat Simulation Theory" | Adversarial dreaming: rehearsing responses to anticipated threats |
   162→| Grassé (1959), Insectes Sociaux 6(1) | Stigmergic coordination — knowledge entries as pheromone deposits |
   163→| Kanerva (2009), Cognitive Computation 1(2) | HDC: 10,240-bit BSC vectors for sub-microsecond similarity search |
   164→| Lacaux et al. (2021), Science Advances | Edison/Dali creative window: hypnagogic onset yields 83% vs 30% creative problem solving |
   165→| Magnin et al. (2010), PNAS | Thalamic deactivation precedes cortical by 8.6 minutes at sleep onset |
   166→| Byrne (2005), The Rational Imagination | Counterfactual reasoning: fault lines (controllable, recent, abnormal actions) |
   167→| Epstude & Roese (2008), Personality and Social Psychology Review | Functional theory of counterfactual thinking: upward counterfactuals drive self-improvement |
   168→| Sumers et al. (2023), arXiv:2309.02427, CoALA framework | Three cognitive speeds: Gamma/Theta/Delta |
   169→| WSCL (2024) | Wake-sleep continual learning: 38% reduction in catastrophic forgetting |
   170→| Simonton (2010), "Creative thought as blind-variation and selective-retention" | BVSR theory: creativity requires variation + selection |
   171→| Gammaitoni et al. (1998), Reviews of Modern Physics | Stochastic resonance: controlled noise improves signal detection |
   172→
   173→---
   174→
   175→## Relationship to Other Cognitive Cross-Cuts
   176→
   177→Dreams interact deeply with the other cognitive cross-cuts:
   178→
   179→- **Neuro (NeuroStore)**: Dreams *consume* episodes and knowledge entries from NeuroStore and *produce* new insights, heuristics, and confidence updates that are written back to NeuroStore. This bidirectional flow is the CLS architecture in action.
   180→
   181→- **Daimon (Affect Engine)**: The Daimon's PAD (Pleasure-Arousal-Dominance) emotional state vectors influence dream processing. High-arousal episodes are prioritized for replay. Emotional depotentiation during REM reduces the Daimon's arousal markers. The Daimon provides somatic markers that weight episode significance.
   182→
   183→- **Hypnagogia**: The hypnagogia engine is a sub-component of the dream system that operates at the transition between waking and sleep. It uses controlled noise injection (stochastic resonance) to generate novel associations that the structured dream phases then process. See [07-hypnagogia-engine.md](07-hypnagogia-engine.md).
   184→
   185→---
   186→
   187→## Implementation Status
   188→
   189→| Component | Status | Location |
   190→|-----------|--------|----------|
   191→| `DreamsEngine` scaffold | Placeholder only | `crates/roko-golem/src/dreams.rs` (43 lines, to be dissolved) |
   192→| `DreamCycle` consolidation engine | **Implemented** | `crates/roko-dreams/src/cycle.rs` |
   193→| `DreamRunner` public facade | **Implemented** | `crates/roko-dreams/src/runner.rs` |
   194→| `DreamLoopConfig` scheduling | **Implemented** | `crates/roko-dreams/src/runner.rs` |
   195→| `PatternMiner` trigram mining | **Implemented** | `crates/roko-learn/src/pattern_discovery.rs` |
   196→| `KMedoids` HDC clustering | **Implemented** | `crates/roko-learn/src/hdc_clustering.rs` |
   197→| Episode replay scheduler (G1) | Not started | Implementation plan §G |
   198→| Counterfactual simulation (G6) | Not started | Implementation plan §G |
   199→| Cross-episode meta-pattern consolidation (G7) | **Implemented** | `crates/roko-learn/src/pattern_discovery.rs` (`CrossEpisodeConsolidator`) |
   200→| Novel strategy generation (G8) | Not started | Implementation plan §G |
   201→
   202→> **Note**: The `roko-golem` crate is being dissolved (see `01-naming-map.md`). The `DreamsEngine` placeholder in `roko-golem/src/dreams.rs` will be deleted. The `roko-dreams` crate is the canonical location for all dream subsystem code. The `HypnagogiaEngine` placeholder in `roko-golem/src/hypnagogia.rs` will move to `roko-dreams` as a `hypnagogia` module.
   203→
   204→---
   205→
   206→## Dream Quality Metrics and Self-Assessment
   207→
   208→Over time, the dream subsystem should be able to evaluate its own effectiveness. Are dreams actually improving waking performance, or are they burning compute for no return? The `DreamQualityDashboard` aggregates quality signals across dream cycles:
   209→
   210→```rust
   211→/// Aggregate dream quality metrics tracked across cycles.
   212→pub struct DreamQualityDashboard {
   213→    /// Cycles tracked.
   214→    pub total_cycles: usize,
   215→    /// Mean promotion rate: fraction of staged hypotheses eventually promoted.
   216→    pub mean_promotion_rate: f64,
   217→    /// Mean diversity: average pairwise HDC distance of generated hypotheses.
   218→    pub mean_hypothesis_diversity: f64,
   219→    /// Mean waking improvement: estimated reduction in task retries attributable to dreams.
   220→    pub mean_waking_improvement: f64,
   221→    /// Nightmare rate: nightmares detected per dream cycle.
   222→    pub nightmare_rate: f64,
   223→    /// Cost efficiency: waking improvement per dollar spent on dreams.
   224→    pub cost_efficiency: f64,
   225→    /// Trend: is dream quality improving, declining, or stable?
   226→    pub trend: DreamQualityTrend,
   227→}
   228→
   229→pub enum DreamQualityTrend {
   230→    Improving { slope: f64 },
   231→    Stable,
   232→    Declining { slope: f64 },
   233→}
   234→```
   235→
   236→Key metrics explained:
   237→
   238→- **Promotion rate** measures whether dreams generate *useful* hypotheses. A low promotion rate (< 0.10) suggests dreams are producing noise. A high rate (> 0.40) suggests dreams may not be exploring broadly enough. The healthy range is 0.15-0.35.
   239→- **Hypothesis diversity** uses pairwise HDC cosine distance to measure whether dreams are exploring diverse territory or converging on the same patterns. Low diversity triggers the Hypnagogia engine's stochastic resonance to inject more noise.
   240→- **Waking improvement** estimates the causal effect of dreams on waking performance by comparing task retry rates and gate pass rates before and after dream consolidation cycles. This is the bottom-line metric: are dreams making the agent better at its job?
   241→- **Nightmare rate** tracks how often the nightmare detection system (see [17-advanced-dream-concepts.md](17-advanced-dream-concepts.md)) fires. A rising nightmare rate may indicate the agent is encountering genuinely novel failure modes that warrant attention.
   242→- **Cost efficiency** normalizes waking improvement by dream compute cost, ensuring that dreams provide positive ROI. If cost efficiency drops below 1.0, the dream scheduler should reduce frequency or switch to cheaper models.
   243→- **Trend** fits a linear regression over the last N cycles (default: 20) to determine whether dream quality is improving, stable, or declining. A declining trend triggers diagnostic logging and may adjust dream parameters (e.g., increasing REM allocation, changing model tier).
   244→
   245→---
   246→
   247→## What to Read Next
   248→
   249→- [01-three-phase-cycle.md](01-three-phase-cycle.md) — The core NREM→REM→Integration cycle with full technical detail
   250→- [02-nrem-replay.md](02-nrem-replay.md) — Mattar-Daw utility-weighted episode selection and replay mechanics
   251→- [03-rem-imagination.md](03-rem-imagination.md) — Pearl SCM counterfactuals and Boden creativity modes
   252→- [04-consolidation-and-staging.md](04-consolidation-and-staging.md) — SQLite staging buffer and confidence-gated promotion
   253→- [07-hypnagogia-engine.md](07-hypnagogia-engine.md) — The four-layer creative onset engine
   254→- [12-sleep-time-compute.md](12-sleep-time-compute.md) — Computational economics of offline processing
   255→

# Dreams: Vision and the Dream-as-Death Reframe

> **Layer**: Cognitive Cross-Cut (injected into L0 Runtime scheduling, L1 Framework agent dispatch, L2 Scaffold context assembly)
>
> **Synapse Traits**: `Substrate` (dream outputs stored in NeuroStore), `Scorer` (replay utility scoring), `Gate` (staging buffer validation), `Policy` (dream scheduling policy)
>
> **Crate**: `roko-dreams` (standalone crate; see `crates/roko-dreams/`)
>
> **Prerequisites**: [01-three-phase-cycle.md](01-three-phase-cycle.md), [../03-neuro/INDEX.md](../06-neuro/INDEX.md) (NeuroStore), [../04-daimon/INDEX.md](../09-daimon/INDEX.md) (affect engine)


> **Implementation**: Scaffold

---

## What Dreams Are in the Roko Architecture

Dreams are a **Delta-frequency cognitive process** — the slowest of three cognitive speeds that govern how a Roko agent thinks. Where Gamma (~5–15 seconds) handles reactive perception-action loops and Theta (~75 seconds) handles strategic re-planning, Delta operates on the scale of hours. Dreams are what happens when an agent has nothing urgent to do: it turns inward, replays its past experiences, imagines counterfactual alternatives, and consolidates the results into durable knowledge.

The term "dream" is not metaphorical. The system implements biological sleep consolidation mechanisms — hippocampal replay, emotional depotentiation, counterfactual simulation — mapped onto LLM-based agents. The research basis is extensive:

- **Complementary Learning Systems (CLS)**: McClelland et al. (1995) demonstrated that biological brains maintain two learning systems — a fast episodic system (hippocampus) and a slow semantic system (neocortex). Dreams are the bridge: during sleep, episodic memories are replayed and gradually integrated into semantic knowledge. Roko's dream system implements this exact architecture, with the episode log as the fast system and NeuroStore as the slow system.

- **Sleep-time compute**: Lin et al. (2025, arXiv:2504.11651) showed that dedicating computation to offline processing yields a 5× reduction in test-time compute requirements. The insight is that agents can do significant cognitive work during idle periods, making their waking performance dramatically more efficient.

- **DreamerV3**: Hafner et al. (2025, Nature) demonstrated that agents learning world models and planning within them can master diverse domains. Roko's dream system uses a similar architecture — the agent builds internal models during dreaming and plans within those models via counterfactual simulation.

- **Generative Agents**: Park et al. (2023, UIST, arXiv:2304.03442) introduced periodic reflection cycles where agents synthesize higher-order observations from raw experiences. Roko's dream consolidation directly implements this pattern.

The Roko dream system produces five categories of output:

| Output | Description | Destination |
|--------|-------------|-------------|
| **Insights** | Patterns extracted from episode replay (e.g., "tasks involving refactoring consistently fail the test gate when the model is under-specified") | NeuroStore via `Substrate` trait |
| **Heuristics** | Promoted insights with 3+ confirmations, becoming actionable rules | NeuroStore + PLAYBOOK.md |
| **Counterfactual strategies** | Novel approaches generated by imagining alternative histories | Staging buffer → NeuroStore on validation |
| **Meta-patterns** | Cross-episode structural similarities discovered via HDC clustering | NeuroStore |
| **Confidence updates** | Strengthened or weakened belief in existing knowledge entries | NeuroStore confidence scores |

---

## The Critical Reframe: Dreams Are NOT Death-Triggered

### What Changed and Why

The legacy Bardo architecture framed dreams as a response to approaching death. In that system, agents had "stochastic death clocks" — random countdowns to mortality — and dream frequency intensified as death approached. The metaphor was evocative but architecturally harmful:

1. **It coupled dreams to mortality**, making the dream subsystem depend on a death-clock subsystem that has no place in a general-purpose agent framework.
2. **It made dream frequency unpredictable** — driven by a PRNG countdown rather than by actual cognitive need.
3. **It excluded non-mortal agents** from dreaming, even though every agent benefits from offline consolidation regardless of whether it has a finite lifespan.
4. **It introduced morbid language** throughout the codebase that made the project harder to explain to investors, researchers, and contributors.

### What Replaces It

In the Roko architecture, dreams are triggered by two mechanisms:

**1. Idle-time triggering**: When an agent has no active tasks and has been idle for a configurable threshold (default: 15 minutes), the dream scheduler fires a consolidation cycle. This is the primary trigger. The logic is simple: if you have nothing to do, process what you've learned.

```rust
pub struct DreamLoopConfig {
    /// Whether automatic dreaming is enabled.
    pub auto_dream: bool,
    /// Idle threshold in minutes before a dream may run.
    pub idle_threshold_mins: u64,
    /// Minimum number of new episodes required before dreaming.
    pub min_episodes_for_dream: usize,
    /// Agent backend used to review the dream batch.
    pub agent: DreamAgentConfig,
}
```

**2. Scheduled consolidation**: The Delta-frequency scheduler can trigger dreams on a fixed schedule (e.g., every 4 hours, or at specific times of day). This ensures that even busy agents with continuous task queues still get periodic consolidation time.

Both triggers are configurable in `roko.toml`:

```toml
[dreams]
auto_dream = true
idle_threshold_mins = 15
min_episodes_for_dream = 5
```

### What the Legacy System Called "Death Dreams"

The legacy PRD described several dream-adjacent concepts in death-related terms. Here is the mapping to their Roko equivalents:

| Legacy Concept | Legacy Description | Roko Equivalent | Roko Description |
|----------------|-------------------|-----------------|------------------|
| **Dream-as-approaching-death** | Dream frequency increases as the agent's death clocks tick down | **Idle-triggered / scheduled consolidation** | Dream frequency is driven by idle time and schedule, not mortality |
| **Terminal dreaming** | Frantic dream cycles every 67 ticks in the Terminal behavioral phase | **Intensive consolidation mode** | When an agent has accumulated many unprocessed episodes (high backlog), consolidation cycles run more frequently — not because of approaching death but because there is more material to process |
| **Death-proximate replay** | Replaying traumatic episodes as the agent nears death | **High-salience replay** | Episodes with high emotional intensity (measured by the Daimon's PAD vectors) are prioritized for replay regardless of the agent's lifecycle state |
| **Thanatopsis dream output** | Final dream producing a "death testament" | **Knowledge export** | An agent can export its accumulated knowledge at any time via user-controlled backup/restore — this is not death-specific |
| **Stochastic death clock** | Random countdown to agent termination that influences dream scheduling | **REMOVED** | No concept of random death in Roko. Agents run until budget exhaustion (economic) or knowledge plateau (epistemic), both of which are continuous metrics, not clocks |
| **Dream urgency from mortality** | Urgency score weighted by proximity to death | **Dream urgency from backlog** | Urgency score weighted by number of unprocessed episodes and time since last consolidation |

### The Underlying Mechanism Is Preserved

The computational mechanisms that made the legacy dream system powerful are fully retained. What changes is *when and why* dreams fire, not *what happens during* a dream:

- **NREM replay** still replays episodes weighted by the Mattar & Daw (2018, Nature Neuroscience) utility formula
- **REM imagination** still generates counterfactuals using Pearl's structural causal models and Boden's three creativity modes
- **Integration** still uses a staging buffer with confidence-gated promotion to permanent knowledge
- **Emotional depotentiation** still reduces arousal markers per Walker & van der Helm (2009, Psychological Bulletin)
- **HDC counterfactual synthesis** still uses 10,240-bit BSC vector permutation operations

The academic foundations are unchanged. The trigger logic is simplified. The death framing is removed.

---

## Where Dreams Fit in the Five-Layer Taxonomy

Dreams are a **cognitive cross-cut** — they are not confined to a single layer but inject behavior across multiple layers:

| Layer | Dream Integration |
|-------|-------------------|
| **L0 Runtime** | Dream scheduling: the Delta-frequency scheduler lives in the runtime and decides when to fire dream cycles based on idle time and backlog |
| **L1 Framework** | Agent dispatch: during dreaming, the framework dispatches a review agent (typically a Haiku-class model for NREM, Sonnet-class for REM) to process the dream batch |
| **L2 Scaffold** | Context assembly: dream outputs (insights, heuristics) are injected into future waking prompts via the NeuroStore retrieval pipeline |
| **L3 Harness** | Gate validation: dream-generated hypotheses enter the staging buffer and must pass validation gates before promotion to permanent knowledge |
| **L4 Orchestration** | Dream scheduling is coordinated with task orchestration — the orchestrator determines idle periods and delegates to the Delta scheduler |

Dreams flow through the Synapse Architecture:

1. **Substrate**: Episode logs and NeuroStore entries are the substrate from which dreams draw raw material
2. **Scorer**: The Mattar-Daw utility formula scores episodes for replay priority
3. **Gate**: The staging buffer's confidence threshold (0.70) gates promotion of dream-generated hypotheses
4. **Router**: Model routing selects the appropriate LLM tier for each dream phase (cheap models for NREM replay, more capable models for REM imagination)
5. **Composer**: Dream outputs are composed into Engrams (currently `Signal` in the codebase — will be renamed to `Engram` in Tier 0D) and written to NeuroStore
6. **Policy**: The dream scheduling policy observes the Engram stream and emits scheduling decisions

---

## Domain-Agnostic Dreaming

Dreams are **not specific to any domain**. The legacy PRD heavily framed dreams in DeFi terms (replaying liquidation cascades, imagining hedging strategies). In the Roko architecture, dreams are domain-agnostic at the kernel level:

**Coding agent example**: A coding agent replays past implementation episodes. During NREM, it reviews tasks where the compile gate failed and extracts patterns ("when I modify error types without updating the match arms downstream, the compile gate always fails"). During REM, it imagines: "what if I had run clippy before the test gate?" The counterfactual generates a process improvement heuristic that enters the staging buffer.

**Research agent example**: A research agent replays past literature review episodes. During NREM, it identifies citation patterns across successful searches. During REM, it combines topics from unrelated research domains to generate novel research hypotheses.

**Chain agent example**: A chain agent (the `roko-chain` domain plugin) replays past transaction episodes. During NREM, it extracts gas price patterns. During REM, it simulates alternative execution strategies.

The dream engine provides the consolidation mechanism. The domain plugin provides the episodes and the interpretation. This separation is enforced by the `roko-dreams` crate's lack of dependency on any domain-specific crate.

---

## Key Academic Citations for the Dream Subsystem

The dream system draws from a rich body of research. These citations appear throughout the sub-docs in this topic folder:

| Paper | Relevance to Dreams |
|-------|-------------------|
| McClelland et al. (1995), Complementary Learning Systems | Foundation: fast episodic + slow semantic memory, bridged by sleep replay |
| Mattar & Daw (2018), Nature Neuroscience, "Prioritized memory access" | NREM replay: utility-weighted episode selection formula |
| Walker & van der Helm (2009), Psychological Bulletin, "Overnight therapy" | Emotional depotentiation: REM sleep reduces emotional charge by 0.3-0.5 per cycle |
| Boden (2004), The Creative Mind: Myths and Mechanisms | REM imagination: three creativity modes (combinational, exploratory, transformational) |
| Pearl (2009), Causality: Models, Reasoning, and Inference | REM counterfactuals: structural causal model three-level framework |
| Hafner et al. (2025), Nature, "DreamerV3" | World model learning during dreaming |
| Park et al. (2023), UIST, arXiv:2304.03442, "Generative Agents" | Periodic reflection and memory synthesis |
| Lin et al. (2025), arXiv:2504.11651, "Sleep-time compute" | 5× reduction in test-time compute via offline processing |
| Revonsuo (2000), Behavioral and Brain Sciences, "Threat Simulation Theory" | Adversarial dreaming: rehearsing responses to anticipated threats |
| Grassé (1959), Insectes Sociaux 6(1) | Stigmergic coordination — knowledge entries as pheromone deposits |
| Kanerva (2009), Cognitive Computation 1(2) | HDC: 10,240-bit BSC vectors for sub-microsecond similarity search |
| Lacaux et al. (2021), Science Advances | Edison/Dali creative window: hypnagogic onset yields 83% vs 30% creative problem solving |
| Magnin et al. (2010), PNAS | Thalamic deactivation precedes cortical by 8.6 minutes at sleep onset |
| Byrne (2005), The Rational Imagination | Counterfactual reasoning: fault lines (controllable, recent, abnormal actions) |
| Epstude & Roese (2008), Personality and Social Psychology Review | Functional theory of counterfactual thinking: upward counterfactuals drive self-improvement |
| Sumers et al. (2023), arXiv:2309.02427, CoALA framework | Three cognitive speeds: Gamma/Theta/Delta |
| WSCL (2024) | Wake-sleep continual learning: 38% reduction in catastrophic forgetting |
| Simonton (2010), "Creative thought as blind-variation and selective-retention" | BVSR theory: creativity requires variation + selection |
| Gammaitoni et al. (1998), Reviews of Modern Physics | Stochastic resonance: controlled noise improves signal detection |

---

## Relationship to Other Cognitive Cross-Cuts

Dreams interact deeply with the other cognitive cross-cuts:

- **Neuro (NeuroStore)**: Dreams *consume* episodes and knowledge entries from NeuroStore and *produce* new insights, heuristics, and confidence updates that are written back to NeuroStore. This bidirectional flow is the CLS architecture in action.

- **Daimon (Affect Engine)**: The Daimon's PAD (Pleasure-Arousal-Dominance) emotional state vectors influence dream processing. High-arousal episodes are prioritized for replay. Emotional depotentiation during REM reduces the Daimon's arousal markers. The Daimon provides somatic markers that weight episode significance.

- **Hypnagogia**: The hypnagogia engine is a sub-component of the dream system that operates at the transition between waking and sleep. It uses controlled noise injection (stochastic resonance) to generate novel associations that the structured dream phases then process. See [07-hypnagogia-engine.md](07-hypnagogia-engine.md).

## Dream Quality Metrics and Self-Assessment

Over time, the dream subsystem should be able to evaluate its own effectiveness. Are dreams actually improving waking performance, or are they burning compute for no return? The `DreamQualityDashboard` aggregates quality signals across dream cycles:

```rust
/// Aggregate dream quality metrics tracked across cycles.
pub struct DreamQualityDashboard {
    /// Cycles tracked.
    pub total_cycles: usize,
    /// Mean promotion rate: fraction of staged hypotheses eventually promoted.
    pub mean_promotion_rate: f64,
    /// Mean diversity: average pairwise HDC distance of generated hypotheses.
    pub mean_hypothesis_diversity: f64,
    /// Mean waking improvement: estimated reduction in task retries attributable to dreams.
    pub mean_waking_improvement: f64,
    /// Nightmare rate: nightmares detected per dream cycle.
    pub nightmare_rate: f64,
    /// Cost efficiency: waking improvement per dollar spent on dreams.
    pub cost_efficiency: f64,
    /// Trend: is dream quality improving, declining, or stable?
    pub trend: DreamQualityTrend,
}

pub enum DreamQualityTrend {
    Improving { slope: f64 },
    Stable,
    Declining { slope: f64 },
}
```

Key metrics explained:

- **Promotion rate** measures whether dreams generate *useful* hypotheses. A low promotion rate (< 0.10) suggests dreams are producing noise. A high rate (> 0.40) suggests dreams may not be exploring broadly enough. The healthy range is 0.15-0.35.
- **Hypothesis diversity** uses pairwise HDC cosine distance to measure whether dreams are exploring diverse territory or converging on the same patterns. Low diversity triggers the Hypnagogia engine's stochastic resonance to inject more noise.
- **Waking improvement** estimates the causal effect of dreams on waking performance by comparing task retry rates and gate pass rates before and after dream consolidation cycles. This is the bottom-line metric: are dreams making the agent better at its job?
- **Nightmare rate** tracks how often the nightmare detection system (see [17-advanced-dream-concepts.md](17-advanced-dream-concepts.md)) fires. A rising nightmare rate may indicate the agent is encountering genuinely novel failure modes that warrant attention.
- **Cost efficiency** normalizes waking improvement by dream compute cost, ensuring that dreams provide positive ROI. If cost efficiency drops below 1.0, the dream scheduler should reduce frequency or switch to cheaper models.
- **Trend** fits a linear regression over the last N cycles (default: 20) to determine whether dream quality is improving, stable, or declining. A declining trend triggers diagnostic logging and may adjust dream parameters (e.g., increasing REM allocation, changing model tier).

1→# The Three-Phase Dream Cycle
     2→
     3→> **Layer**: Cognitive Cross-Cut (L0 scheduling + L1 agent dispatch + L2 context assembly)
     4→>
     5→> **Synapse Traits**: `Scorer` (Mattar-Daw utility scoring), `Gate` (staging buffer validation), `Composer` (Engram assembly from dream outputs)
     6→>
     7→> **Crate**: `roko-dreams` — `cycle.rs`, `runner.rs`
     8→>
     9→> **Prerequisites**: [00-vision-and-dream-as-death-reframe.md](00-vision-and-dream-as-death-reframe.md)
    10→
    11→
    12→> **Implementation**: Scaffold
    13→
    14→---
    15→
    16→## Overview
    17→
    18→Every dream cycle in Roko consists of three sequential phases, inspired by biological mammalian sleep:
    19→
    20→1. **NREM Replay** — Replaying past experiences with minor mutations to consolidate memory
    21→2. **REM Imagination** — Generating counterfactual scenarios and novel strategies through creative recombination
    22→3. **Integration** — Evaluating dream outputs, updating the staging buffer, and promoting validated hypotheses to permanent knowledge
    23→
    24→These phases are not metaphorical labels. Each phase implements a distinct computational process with its own academic foundations, model requirements, and output types. The three-phase structure is grounded in the Complementary Learning Systems (CLS) theory of McClelland et al. (1995) and the specific consolidation mechanisms documented by Diekelmann & Born (2010, Psychological Review).
    25→
    26→---
    27→
    28→## Phase 1: NREM Replay
    29→
    30→**Purpose**: Strengthen significant memories, weaken irrelevant ones, and extract cross-episode patterns.
    31→
    32→**Biological basis**: During non-REM sleep (particularly slow-wave sleep stages N2-N3), the hippocampus replays compressed versions of recent experiences at accelerated timescales. Sharp-wave ripples coordinate the transfer of episodic memories from hippocampus to neocortex (Buzsáki 1989; Ji & Wilson 2007). The replay is not veridical — it includes minor mutations that test whether patterns hold under perturbation.
    33→
    34→**Computational implementation**: The NREM replay phase selects episodes from the episode log using the Mattar & Daw (2018, Nature Neuroscience) utility formula:
    35→
    36→```
    37→Utility(episode) = Gain × Need × (1 / spacing_penalty)
    38→```
    39→
    40→Where:
    41→- **Gain** measures how much the agent's behavior would improve by better processing this episode. Episodes where the outcome was surprising (the agent's prediction error was large) have high gain.
    42→- **Need** measures how often the agent encounters situations similar to this episode. Episodes from frequently encountered task types have high need.
    43→- **Spacing penalty** implements spacing effects (Cepeda et al. 2006) — recently replayed episodes are penalized to prevent over-rehearsal.
    44→
    45→The selected episodes are replayed with two types of mutation:
    46→
    47→| Mutation Type | Description | Research Basis | Frequency |
    48→|---------------|-------------|----------------|-----------|
    49→| **Perturbation** | Key values within the episode are shifted within a plausible range (e.g., a parameter that was `0.30` becomes `0.25`) | Perturbed replay from hippocampal studies | 30% of replays |
    50→| **Bidirectional replay** | Episodes are replayed both forward (cause→effect) and backward (effect→cause) to strengthen causal associations in both directions | Ambrose et al. (2016), Reverse Replay | All replays |
    51→
    52→The replay phase uses a **Haiku-class model** (cheap, fast) because it primarily involves pattern matching and comparison against existing knowledge, not creative generation.
    53→
    54→> **Full detail**: [02-nrem-replay.md](02-nrem-replay.md)
    55→
    56→---
    57→
    58→## Phase 2: REM Imagination
    59→
    60→**Purpose**: Generate novel strategies and counterfactual hypotheses through creative recombination of existing knowledge.
    61→
    62→**Biological basis**: During REM sleep, the brain enters a state of high-level cortical activation without sensory input. The prefrontal cortex (executive control) is suppressed while associative cortex is active, enabling novel combinations of memories that would be inhibited during waking (Hobson & Schredl 2011). Walker & van der Helm (2009, Psychological Bulletin) demonstrated that REM sleep specifically reduces the emotional charge of traumatic memories — "overnight therapy."
    63→
    64→**Computational implementation**: The REM phase operates in three creativity modes, following Boden's (2004) taxonomy:
    65→
    66→| Creativity Mode | Operation | Prompt Structure | Example |
    67→|----------------|-----------|-----------------|---------|
    68→| **Combinational** | Combine elements from unrelated episodes to discover unexpected similarities | "Given episode A about [topic X] and episode B about [topic Y], what structural patterns do they share?" | Noticing that gas price spikes and governance vote deadlines share the same timing pattern |
    69→| **Exploratory** | Traverse the boundaries of existing strategy spaces, pushing parameters to extremes | "Your current heuristic says [X]. What happens at the extreme of this rule? When would it break?" | Testing whether a "always retry failed tasks 3 times" heuristic still works at 10 retries |
    70→| **Transformational** | Violate fundamental assumptions of existing strategies to generate genuinely novel approaches | "What if [core assumption] were false? What strategy would you use instead?" | Imagining that compilation errors are actually test failures — what would the response be? |
    71→
    72→The REM phase also implements **counterfactual reasoning** via Pearl's (2009) three-level structural causal model (SCM) framework:
    73→
    74→1. **Association** (Level 1): What correlates with what in the episode data?
    75→2. **Intervention** (Level 2): What would happen if the agent had taken a different action?
    76→3. **Counterfactual** (Level 3): Given what actually happened, what would have happened if conditions had been different?
    77→
    78→Byrne's (2005, The Rational Imagination) "fault lines" guide which counterfactuals the agent explores first:
    79→- **Controllable actions**: Things the agent could have done differently (highest priority)
    80→- **Recent actions**: Temporally proximate decisions (second priority)
    81→- **Abnormal actions**: Decisions that deviated from the agent's usual patterns (third priority)
    82→
    83→Epstude & Roese (2008, Personality and Social Psychology Review) provide the functional theory: **upward counterfactuals** ("what if I had done better?") drive self-improvement, while **downward counterfactuals** ("what if I had done worse?") serve as rehearsal for future threats.
    84→
    85→**Emotional depotentiation**: During REM processing, Walker & van der Helm (2009) showed that the emotional charge of memories decreases by 0.3–0.5 units per cycle (on a 0–1 arousal scale). This is implemented as a direct update to the Daimon's PAD arousal dimension for each episode processed:
    86→
    87→```
    88→post_dream_arousal = pre_dream_arousal - depotentiation_delta
    89→depotentiation_delta ∈ [0.3, 0.5] per cycle
    90→```
    91→
    92→The REM phase uses a **Sonnet-class model** (more capable, more expensive) because creative recombination requires genuine reasoning rather than pattern matching.
    93→
    94→> **Full detail**: [03-rem-imagination.md](03-rem-imagination.md)
    95→
    96→---
    97→
    98→## Phase 3: Integration
    99→
   100→**Purpose**: Evaluate the outputs from NREM replay and REM imagination, stage validated hypotheses, and promote them to permanent knowledge.
   101→
   102→**Biological basis**: The integration phase corresponds to the brief waking periods between sleep cycles (interspersed micro-arousals) and the consolidation that occurs during the transition between NREM and REM. During these transitions, the brain evaluates which memories have been sufficiently strengthened and which should decay (Stickgold & Walker 2013).
   103→
   104→**Computational implementation**: Integration is a **pure computation phase** — no LLM call is required. It operates on the outputs from NREM and REM:
   105→
   106→### The Staging Buffer
   107→
   108→Dream-generated hypotheses enter a SQLite staging buffer at confidence level 0.20–0.30. This is the "maybe" zone — the hypothesis is interesting enough to record but not validated enough to act on.
   109→
   110→```sql
   111→CREATE TABLE staged_hypotheses (
   112→    id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
   113→    content TEXT NOT NULL,
   114→    confidence REAL DEFAULT 0.25,
   115→    source_phase TEXT NOT NULL,        -- 'nrem' or 'rem'
   116→    source_episodes TEXT,              -- JSON array of episode IDs
   117→    created_at INTEGER NOT NULL,       -- Unix timestamp
   118→    last_validated_at INTEGER,
   119→    validation_count INTEGER DEFAULT 0,
   120→    status TEXT DEFAULT 'staged'       -- staged | validated | promoted | expired | refuted
   121→);
   122→```
   123→
   124→### Confidence Ladder
   125→
   126→Hypotheses climb a confidence ladder through waking validation:
   127→
   128→| Confidence Range | Status | What Happens |
   129→|-----------------|--------|--------------|
   130→| 0.20–0.30 | **Staged** | Hypothesis just entered from a dream. No action taken yet. |
   131→| 0.30–0.50 | **Partially Validated** | Some waking evidence supports the hypothesis. Confidence boosted by 0.10 per confirmation. |
   132→| 0.50–0.70 | **Strongly Supported** | Multiple confirmations. The agent begins to act on this hypothesis tentatively. |
   133→| ≥ 0.70 | **Promoted** | Hypothesis is written to permanent NeuroStore as a validated insight or heuristic. |
   134→
   135→### Promotion to Permanent Knowledge
   136→
   137→When a hypothesis reaches confidence ≥ 0.70, the integration phase promotes it:
   138→
   139→1. The hypothesis is written to NeuroStore as a `KnowledgeEntry` with `source: "dream"` provenance
   140→2. If the hypothesis represents a new heuristic (actionable rule), it is also written to `PLAYBOOK.md`
   141→3. The staging buffer entry is updated to `status: 'promoted'`
   142→4. A `DreamOutcomeEvent` is emitted for downstream listeners
   143→
   144→### Expiration
   145→
   146→Hypotheses that are not validated within a configurable window (default: 5,000 ticks / ~3.5 days) expire:
   147→
   148→1. The staging buffer entry is updated to `status: 'expired'`
   149→2. The hypothesis enters normal temporal decay in NeuroStore if it was already partially stored
   150→3. No action is taken — the hypothesis was not confirmed by waking experience
   151→
   152→### What Integration Produces
   153→
   154→| Output | Description | Destination |
   155→|--------|-------------|-------------|
   156→| Promoted insights | Validated hypotheses that became permanent knowledge | NeuroStore (`Substrate`) |
   157→| Updated confidence scores | Existing knowledge entries whose confidence was adjusted by dream replay | NeuroStore |
   158→| Emotional depotentiation | Reduced arousal scores for processed episodes | Daimon PAD update |
   159→| Meta-patterns | Cross-episode structural similarities discovered by HDC clustering | NeuroStore |
   160→| Dream report | A structured `DreamCycleReport` summarizing the cycle | `.roko/dreams/dream-{timestamp}.json` |
   161→
   162→> **Full detail**: [04-consolidation-and-staging.md](04-consolidation-and-staging.md)
   163→
   164→---
   165→
   166→## Dream Cycle State Machine
   167→
   168→The dream cycle progresses through a deterministic state machine:
   169→
   170→```
   171→IDLE → NREM_REPLAY → REM_IMAGINATION → INTEGRATION → IDLE
   172→```
   173→
   174→Each state transition is logged. The agent cannot be interrupted mid-phase (the current phase runs to completion before any transition). Between full dream cycles, the agent may enter a micro-consolidation mode where only the most urgent single replay is processed — this handles the case where the agent is briefly idle between tasks but not idle enough for a full dream cycle.
   175→
   176→```rust
   177→pub enum DreamPhase {
   178→    /// No dream in progress. Agent is in waking mode.
   179→    Idle,
   180→    /// NREM replay: re-processing past episodes with mutations.
   181→    NremReplay {
   182→        episodes_to_replay: usize,
   183→        episodes_replayed: usize,
   184→    },
   185→    /// REM imagination: counterfactual generation and creative recombination.
   186→    RemImagination {
   187→        counterfactuals_to_generate: usize,
   188→        counterfactuals_generated: usize,
   189→    },
   190→    /// Integration: evaluating outputs, staging, promoting.
   191→    Integration {
   192→        hypotheses_to_evaluate: usize,
   193→        hypotheses_evaluated: usize,
   194→    },
   195→}
   196→```
   197→
   198→---
   199→
   200→## Resource Allocation Across Phases
   201→
   202→Each phase has different computational requirements:
   203→
   204→| Phase | Model Tier | Context Window | Typical Duration | Cost Profile |
   205→|-------|-----------|----------------|------------------|-------------|
   206→| **NREM Replay** | Haiku-class (T0) | Minimal — episode data + current heuristics | 60–120 seconds for a batch of 10 episodes | Low (~$0.001/episode) |
   207→| **REM Imagination** | Sonnet-class (T1) | Full — needs broad knowledge context for creative recombination | 120–300 seconds for 3–5 counterfactuals | Medium (~$0.01/counterfactual) |
   208→| **Integration** | None (pure computation) | N/A — operates on structured data only | < 5 seconds | Negligible |
   209→
   210→The asymmetry is deliberate. NREM replay is cheap pattern matching — a fast, inexpensive model suffices. REM imagination requires genuine reasoning and creativity — a more capable model is worth the cost. Integration is arithmetic and database operations — no model needed at all.
   211→
   212→The CascadeRouter (see `crates/roko-learn/src/cascade_router.rs`) handles model selection for each phase. During dreaming, the agent signals `SIGPAUSE` to any Gamma-frequency processes, allowing the Delta-frequency dream cycle to consume the compute budget.
   213→
   214→---
   215→
   216→## Concurrent Execution
   217→
   218→Dream phases execute sequentially within a single dream cycle, but multiple aspects of NREM replay can run concurrently:
   219→
   220→- Multiple episodes can be replayed in parallel (each replay is independent)
   221→- Replays do not share state until the aggregation step at the end of NREM
   222→- REM imagination is sequential (each counterfactual builds on previous context)
   223→- Integration is a single-threaded batch operation
   224→
   225→The `DreamCycle` implementation in `crates/roko-dreams/src/cycle.rs` manages this concurrency.
   226→
   227→---
   228→
   229→## Dream Scheduling
   230→
   231→The dream scheduler determines when and how often to fire dream cycles:
   232→
   233→```rust
   234→impl DreamRunner {
   235→    pub fn schedule(&self) -> Option<Duration> {
   236→        if !self.config.auto_dream {
   237→            return None;
   238→        }
   239→
   240→        // Count episodes since last dream
   241→        let recent_episodes = count_episodes_since_last_dream();
   242→        if recent_episodes < self.config.min_episodes_for_dream {
   243→            return None;
   244→        }
   245→
   246→        // Check idle threshold
   247→        let last_activity = most_recent_episode_timestamp();
   248→        let idle_duration = now - last_activity;
   249→        if idle_duration >= self.config.idle_threshold_mins {
   250→            return Some(Duration::ZERO); // Dream now
   251→        }
   252→
   253→        // Schedule for when idle threshold will be reached
   254→        let remaining = self.config.idle_threshold_mins - idle_duration;
   255→        Some(remaining)
   256→    }
   257→}
   258→```
   259→
   260→Key scheduling parameters:
   261→
   262→| Parameter | Default | Description |
   263→|-----------|---------|-------------|
   264→| `auto_dream` | `true` | Whether idle-triggered dreaming is enabled |
   265→| `idle_threshold_mins` | `15` | Minutes of inactivity before triggering a dream |
   266→| `min_episodes_for_dream` | `5` | Minimum unprocessed episodes required before a dream can fire |
   267→
   268→The scheduler interacts with the L4 Orchestration layer: the plan executor knows when the agent is between tasks and can signal the dream scheduler that an idle period is beginning.
   269→
   270→---
   271→
   272→## Dream Cycle Output: The DreamCycleReport
   273→
   274→Every completed dream cycle produces a `DreamCycleReport` — a structured JSON document persisted to `.roko/dreams/`:
   275→
   276→```rust
   277→pub struct DreamCycleReport {
   278→    /// When the dream cycle started.
   279→    pub started_at: DateTime<Utc>,
   280→    /// When the dream cycle completed.
   281→    pub completed_at: DateTime<Utc>,
   282→    /// Timestamp of the most recent episode processed.
   283→    pub processed_through: Option<DateTime<Utc>>,
   284→    /// Number of episodes replayed during NREM.
   285→    pub episodes_replayed: usize,
   286→    /// Number of counterfactuals generated during REM.
   287→    pub counterfactuals_generated: usize,
   288→    /// Insights extracted during the cycle.
   289→    pub insights: Vec<InsightRecord>,
   290→    /// Patterns discovered via cross-episode consolidation.
   291→    pub patterns: Vec<PatternRecord>,
   292→    /// Hypotheses staged for future validation.
   293→    pub staged_hypotheses: usize,
   294→    /// Hypotheses promoted to permanent knowledge.
   295→    pub promoted_hypotheses: usize,
   296→    /// Confidence updates applied to existing knowledge.
   297→    pub confidence_updates: usize,
   298→    /// Emotional depotentiation summary.
   299→    pub depotentiation: DepotentiationSummary,
   300→}
   301→```
   302→
   303→Reports are stored as `dream-{unix_timestamp_ms}.json` in `.roko/dreams/`. The `DreamRunner::latest_report()` method retrieves the most recent one.
   304→
   305→---
   306→
   307→## Biological Fidelity: The Four-Phase Sleep Architecture
   308→
   309→The three-phase cycle (NREM → REM → Integration) maps cleanly to biological sleep but omits a critical fourth element: the **transition phases**. Diekelmann & Born (2010) and the WSCL computational model (Skenderi et al., 2024) both demonstrate that the transitions between phases are not dead time — they are computationally active periods where the brain/model switches processing modes.
   310→
   311→In Roko, the hypnagogia engine (see [07-hypnagogia-engine.md](07-hypnagogia-engine.md)) serves as the WAKE→NREM transition. A complementary REM→WAKE transition could serve as a "dream integration review" — a brief metacognitive pass where the agent evaluates what it learned from the dream before fully resuming waking operations.
   312→
   313→```rust
   314→/// Extended dream cycle with transition phases.
   315→pub enum ExtendedDreamPhase {
   316→    /// No dream in progress.
   317→    Idle,
   318→    /// Transition: waking → sleep. Hypnagogia engine runs here.
   319→    HypnagogicTransition {
   320→        fragments_generated: usize,
   321→        fragments_retained: usize,
   322→    },
   323→    /// NREM replay phase.
   324→    NremReplay {
   325→        episodes_to_replay: usize,
   326→        episodes_replayed: usize,
   327→    },
   328→    /// Transition: NREM → REM. Episode clusters finalized, seeds selected.
   329→    NremToRemTransition,
   330→    /// REM imagination phase.
   331→    RemImagination {
   332→        counterfactuals_to_generate: usize,
   333→        counterfactuals_generated: usize,
   334→    },
   335→    /// Integration phase.
   336→    Integration {
   337→        hypotheses_to_evaluate: usize,
   338→        hypotheses_evaluated: usize,
   339→    },
   340→    /// Transition: sleep → waking. Metacognitive dream review.
   341→    HypnopompicTransition {
   342→        insights_summarized: usize,
   343→    },
   344→}
   345→```
   346→
   347→---
   348→
   349→## Micro-Consolidation: Between-Task Dream Fragments
   350→
   351→Short idle gaps (2-5 minutes) between tasks can support micro-consolidation — a single high-priority episode replay without the full dream cycle. Unlike a full NREM→REM→Integration sequence, micro-consolidation replays only the highest-utility episode (by Mattar-Daw scoring) and does not enter the REM imagination phase. This allows the agent to strengthen its most critical recent memory without the overhead of a complete dream cycle.
   352→
   353→Micro-consolidation is triggered when the agent detects an idle gap that meets the minimum duration but falls short of the full `idle_threshold_mins` required for a complete dream cycle. Because micro-consolidation skips REM and integration, it cannot generate new hypotheses or promote knowledge — it can only reinforce existing episodic memory.
   354→
   355→```rust
   356→/// Micro-consolidation for brief idle gaps.
   357→pub struct MicroConsolidation {
   358→    /// Minimum idle duration to trigger micro-consolidation (seconds).
   359→    pub min_idle_secs: u64,               // default: 120, range: 60-300
   360→    /// Maximum episodes to replay in micro-consolidation.
   361→    pub max_micro_replays: usize,         // default: 1, range: 1-3
   362→    /// Whether micro-consolidation can stage hypotheses.
   363→    pub can_stage: bool,                  // default: false
   364→    /// Model tier for micro-consolidation.
   365→    pub model_tier: ModelTier,            // default: T0 (Haiku-class)
   366→}
   367→```
   368→
   369→---
   370→
   371→## Cross-References
   372→
   373→| Document | Relevance |
   374→|----------|-----------|
   375→| [02-nrem-replay.md](02-nrem-replay.md) | Full detail on Mattar-Daw utility scoring, bidirectional replay, perturbed replay |
   376→| [03-rem-imagination.md](03-rem-imagination.md) | Full detail on Pearl SCM, Boden creativity modes, emotional depotentiation |
   377→| [04-consolidation-and-staging.md](04-consolidation-and-staging.md) | Full detail on staging buffer, confidence ladder, promotion mechanics |
   378→| [05-dream-evolution.md](05-dream-evolution.md) | The fourth phase (EVOLUTION): memetic selection and strategy evolution |
   379→| [12-sleep-time-compute.md](12-sleep-time-compute.md) | Computational economics and budget allocation |
   380→| [13-scheduling-and-triggers.md](13-scheduling-and-triggers.md) | Detailed scheduling logic and trigger conditions |
   381→
